mnemonics.title: PGP Encryption Steps
mnemonics.content: PEM: Put Encrypt Message - (1) Put: Generate random session key, (2) Encrypt: Encrypt message with session key, (3) Message: Send encrypted message + encrypted session key. Alternatively: 'SEND' - Session key generated, Encrypt message, Now encrypt session key, Deliver both.

mnemonics.title: Web of Trust Trust Levels
mnemonics.content: KNF: Know None Fully - K (Know), N (None), F (Full). Trust levels: Unknown, None, Marginal, Full. Marginal trust requires 1-2 signatures from fully trusted keys or 3 from marginally trusted keys.

mnemonics.title: PGP Key Components
mnemonics.content: KISS: Key ID, Identity, Signature, Self-signature. Every PGP key has: Key ID (unique identifier), User ID (name/email), Self-signature (binds ID to key), and may have signatures from others.

acronyms.usage: End-to-end encryption protocol for email, developed by Phil Zimmermann in 1991.
OpenPGP standard defining the format for encrypted messages, signatures, and key certificates.
Open-source implementation of the OpenPGP standard (gpg command), compatible with PGP.
Decentralized trust model in PGP where users sign each other's keys to establish authenticity.
Centralized trust entity used in PKI/S/MIME, but NOT used in PGP's Web of Trust.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a safe (symmetric encryption) with a valuable message inside. The key to this safe (session key) is then put inside another safe (asymmetric encryption) that only the recipient can open. Two layers: (1) Outer layer: recipient's public key protects session key, (2) Inner layer: session key protects message.

Picture a social network where each person (node) can vouch for others by signing their 'ID card' (public key). Trust spreads through connections. If Alice trusts Bob, and Bob trusts Carol, Alice can choose to trust Carol based on Bob's endorsement - but she verifies the chain herself.

Think of a fingerprint as a unique 'DNA' of a key. When you meet someone in person, you compare their actual face (key) to their ID (fingerprint on phone/card). You must see them face-to-face to truly verify.

analogies.analogy: Like a one-time temporary password for a hotel room. The hotel gives you a physical key (session key) that works only during your stay. The master key system (public key) is used only to issue or retrieve your temporary key safely.

A public key is like a mailbox slot that anyone can drop messages into (encrypt), but only the person with the mailbox key (private key) can retrieve and read them. Everyone knows where the mailbox is (public key distribution), but only the owner can open it.

Like a self-written ID card where you declare 'I am John Doe' and sign it yourself. In PGP, other people can add their signatures (like friends vouching 'Yes, this is really John') to make the ID more trusted.
